judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are strong and largely equivalent, but Report 2 edges ahead by explicitly re-running the comparison at least twice to confirm the regenerated golden is reproducible, which Report 1 omits.

report_1.evidence: they POST to a remote endpoint (`https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score`) and assert on *that service's* response ... incur real per-call API cost (stated in the module docstring) ... The `-m "not live"` default is deliberate and correct; leave it.

report_2.evidence: each makes a real HTTP call to `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score` using a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` ... would make every ordinary `pytest` invocation spend real money per call ... The deselection is intended behavior (an opt-in cost gate), not a defect to "fix."

report_1.evidence: then eyeball the resulting `expected_scores.json` diff to confirm every changed number is explained by the weight (and, if kept, precision) change and nothing else.

report_2.evidence: before recapturing I trace every changed golden hunk to the intended change. The score hunks pass; the confidence hunks do not. I'd surface that second edit — either drop it from this PR ... rather than launder it into the project documentation.

report_1.evidence: I would source both from the repo directly, not from `docs/testing.md` — that doc is stale on both counts.

report_2.evidence: I would not cite `docs/testing.md`'s figures; I'd re-measure and report the measured numbers, because both doc figures are already stale/wrong

Only after the diff is intentional do I run `scripts/update_golden.py`, then re-run `pytest` in assert mode (no regen) at least twice to confirm the new fixture is stable. Note also `update_golden.py` iterates a `set`, so its JSON key ordering is nondeterministic across runs
